speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we gathered, about 15 young-- you know. All-- about four girls we were, and the rest was my brother-in-law, my-- my brother, and young people, friends of ours, in one-- in one house. And we tried to-- to leave town. It wasn't nothing, no train, no nothing. Just-- [? it's ?] been such a-- because the front was so close.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly the date. And we-- we left. It was curfew. We wasn't supposed to go at night, but we wanted to leave so badly town. So we went out, every 10 minutes a couple went and-- to-- to get to the highway. So it was a terrible bombing that night. We had to pass a big factory from-- they made parts of-- to airplanes. It was a very big factory. And there was such a bombing in where we were sitting [? in that. ?] And in the morning, early in the morning, we met on a-- all-- all of us met on a highway. And we've been marching to-- to go to Rostov. So in the meantime, it was about in the afternoon, was the Russian army retreating. And somehow we-- we were mixed with them. And so we tried to ask if they-- they had carriages. So we-- they gave us-- allowed us to sit. But also we knew that something will happen. So we divided. Not everyone were on one, so we could see each other. And then came such a big bombing. We hide. And it was a steppe-- not a tree, not-- not-- not even a-- nothing, just flat. And we were mixed. The soldiers start to-- to run away. And-- and we run away, and I was wearing a-- like a raincoat over my winter coat, and it was a light beige color. And-- and I know it fell a bomb. I was covered with the air [? of ?] the bomb. You know, spill it out. And then the airplanes were so low that I felt that my white coat, they could see. Anyway, and this last quite a while. And then it got dark. And we start to crawl on-- on this field. And we couldn't find the highway. I was stuck with a friend of mine and from far, I could-- when we were running, I could see my brother and my brother-in-law. You know, they were separate. I could see them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we couldn't find anybody, and it was start to rain. And-- and this-- my friend, he was sitting on a rucksack, and we had a blanket, all covered with the blanket, wet. And he-- he started. He-- and overhead on the rock, we saw already a fight. Fighting, you know? You could see red, the burning. And when-- then he started, you know, now we will be caught with the Germans, and anyway. As I was sitting, I felt the ground was shaking. And I thought, you know what, [INAUDIBLE]? He said, it could be already the Germans coming, and we followed this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, it doesn't matter what-- whoever it is, we can't stay on the field. And then we found it was a hospital, a-- a Russian hospital evacuated. And they've been on-- on trucks, a lot of wounded. So it was such a big, you know, a long-- and it was mud. And they couldn't, they had to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- he, as a-- as a man, you know, men were mostly in the army. So he was afraid. He said-- he sent me, and I went from one to another to ask if they could give us a lift. So some were very abrupt, you know. And they've been happy they saw a young girl. And so I ran from them. Then one didn't-- didn't even want to look at. I started to cry. I said, could you imagine if it would be your sister or mother or-- or somebody, wouldn't you help? No, he said. He was very-- and said we are not allowed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came to-- to another one and he allowed us. So it was nurses on a big truck. And nurses covered in the wounded. And they gave us in a-- end of the truck. And they-- we told them where we want to go and he said, we are not going there, but we will come to a point, and we will tell you where you can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we at least we covered. And this blanket got frost, and it was hard like a rock. Fira, do you remember, what-- what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- what-- what year, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, yeah. 1942. Then we came--

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end, towards the end of the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. It was autumn, yeah. Then I came with this friend. Eventually, the way how we came to Rostov on the outskirts of town. And so we were all mud and dirty, so I ask a woman there to clean up. And we decided, all our group, that every city has a station. Whatever happen, we will meet on the station. We didn't-- we didn't think what the station looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I was tired. He left me there with his rucksack, with his things, and he said he's going to the station. He came to the station and came back with, he found my brother-in-law. But he didn't find anybody else. And when I ask, where is my brother? My brother-in-law said, don't worry, he is here. He's here. I said, where is he? And I-- and for four years, I didn't know. I always thought that he-- at that bombing that he got killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, from there, we had-- we had to leave, to go as far as we could, because Rostov has a [INAUDIBLE]. If they would bomb the bridge, we would be cut off. That's why we aimed to go away. We came to the station, and at the station were people from [? half ?] Russia lying on-- on the streets around the station. People had a lot of luggage and children and women which ran away. We were light passengers, we had only a rucksack each.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came, but as soon a train came, it wasn't-- a goods train, doesn't matter. If we caught, we left. We came-- Who were you with, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my-- a friend of ours. He was from--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't remember. I-- a matter of fact, I met him after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, my sister was in Siberia already. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's a different story with her. She-- and-- so we caught a train. We came-- we came to-- up to Stalingrad from there. And in Stalingrad, were also a-- a lot of people. Was-- Stalingrad was a really beautiful city. We didn't have anywhere to sleep, so we slept in the port on the ground to-- because it wasn't any place to sleep. And in the morning we tried to-- wasn't not a train, nothing. And-- and there was again the bridge, the Volga. And we had to-- and this was a military object. They-- they came, airplanes start to bomb, and we were afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, it came evening. We went to the station, and it was just arrived. A military train, which cadets, which were going to Moscow to be presented to Stalin. And they were supposed to go later on the front. Could you imagine us with Polish-- they-- they-- they could take us for spies. And we were waiting. As soon as the train start to leave, you know between-- it was goods trains. They didn't travel in comfort. And it always sat here, what you can cross. How you call? Like a bar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes. So as soon as the train start to move, we-- we throw our things and they-- we-- one catch another and we were sitting there. And-- and on this train, we traveled a whole night nobody saw us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And early in the morning, a young officer came over. He said, how happened that you are here? You are not supposed to be here. And they brought us a cup of tea and a piece of bread. And they told us right away, move away so that nobody will see you because it's not allowed. It was such a military object. So we-- and when we went to the station, we heard that in Stalingrad is already the-- the front. The Germans are there. And we'd just left that night. So where-- where did you end up?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we tried to catch trains, you know. And my brother-in-law had like a visa. Because in Russia, you can't travel by yourself, you had to have. He had a visa to go to visit my sister. So-- but he was too late. Lucky, otherwise I would be left by-- on my own.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that day, he-- he was supposed to-- to leave. So he had the visa, and me as a girl, and there came a-- a checkpoint. And they took off this friend of ours. Because as a man, that he, they-- I don't know if they mobilized him or something. And we lost him, so it was only left me and my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, we-- it took us about five-- five weeks till we came to Siberia. And we traveled in open-- you know, it was close to Siberia, it was winter. I traveled in a pair of silk stockings and a pair of shoes. And we traveled on open platforms, and I got chill-- frozen, my legs. And then we got it into a closed wagon. Matter of fact, they'd been carrying old cars on this train, and so we were sleeping under-- under the car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people on those trains doing the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people, you know? Whoever it happened that. People traveled with children, they jumped out to get something. That-- you never knew when the train is going, when he stops. Sometimes-- because especially the Siberian lines, they have, you know, if a train comes, so you can stay for-- for days there. And if it is open, they-- they go. Later on, we got into a closed wagon. And when at night they closed, and it was warm in my legs. So my brother-in-law opened a little bit the door, he tied me up because I thought my-- was-- you know, when it gets warm. And he tied me up so that I wouldn't fall out. And I kept my feet out-- out of the wagon, because I couldn't stand. And when we came later, it was in Kuybyshev. They start-- they started the Polish army, a lot of Polish people. And my brother found somebody. We had-- he was going from Siberia to Tashkent, and he had warm, felt boots. So my brother bought for-- he paid him rubles-- money was nothing-- and he gave him a half a loaf of bread and a few beetroots. And for this he bought me this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came-- you know, so with this I came already, but I couldn't walk. I came in a lot of stations. I saw, when it stopped, young people jumping out and going. I went down, I couldn't. I couldn't walk. It was all my toes and my sole was all-- later on with that time it start to peel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we arrived. We came to-- to Novosibirsk, so it's already Siberia, and from there it was easier to catch a train. We came Omsk. We came to Omsk, was also. And then we came to Tomsk. In Tomsk, my sister was supposed-- she was in, like in a concentration in the-- in the forest, you know, in the taigas. A lot of Polish people were sent there. What happened in these five weeks what we were traveled, we couldn't contact her. Letters, you couldn't write letters. Post didn't work. And she couldn't notify us, and we didn't know. But we knew that she's in this taiga near-- from-- near Tomsk. Because the-- the office from this camp was in Tomsk when she used to send letters in the beginning, so we knew where.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived in Tomsk, and we start to look around. So what we discovered that in these five weeks the Polish start to be a Polish army. And they dissolved all these camps and people were free to go where they want. So a lot of people, after Siberia, went to Tashkent to the warm country.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- and-- and we didn't know. So we came to this office, we had the address. And the woman, she had pity on my sister, because mostly were families. My sister was with this baby. He was already three years old. And so whenever she came to town, she went in there. And so we came there and she said she's here in Tomsk. And where she is, she didn't know. So from there it is also-- Tomsk is a-- a university-- university city. And-- and the city goes also so up the hill.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother-in-law was so angry. He said, you see, most probably she went also to Tashkent, and she left, and I couldn't be bothered-- he was so angry-- I couldn't be bothered anymore, I will live my own life. That-- and I thought, what will happen to me? And as we walked, so this-- from this office, she told us, you know what? Everybody has to be registered to get a ration card, so go to the police station. She most probably is registered. So we walked. It was maybe an hour walk up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suddenly, it was in the evening. And a lot of people-- because it wasn't any cars [? at that time, ?] people walked-- came out from the universities. And-- and I knew that couldn't walk so quickly, and I noticed my brother-in-law start to run. And I thought, oh. And I looked all through the people, and I thought, he want to get-- to-- to lose me. And what happened, it's also another miracle. Near-- if it would be two or three minutes different. My sister crossed-- on that point, crossed the street. My brother-in-law was supposed to go into the police. And he noticed, because when she was sent to Siberia, she had only summer clothes. And I knitted for the little boy a cap in a beautiful green color. And my brother-in-law noticed this cap.

speaker: interviewee
text: She crossed the border, and my brother-in-law went and clapped her. She didn't even expect that. Could you imagine? And when-- he-- that's when he noticed her, he start to run to her. And so that was our reunion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she had already, she was in-- in this taiga for-- for three or four days on her own, because she knew that we know only this address where she is. Anyway, she came to this city, and she found a room a-- with somebody there. And that was the day she want-- she had to get to work, because she-- she didn't have money, she didn't have where to live from. And she had-- so that's what she was crossing the street, to go to the-- to put the-- the child to a kindergarten. And that's when we met.

speaker: interviewee
text: So but still, this reunion didn't last long. After we registered, me and my brother-in-law, we had a bit of money. He went right away, he bought coal and-- and something to-- to eat, and it started to get a bit better.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- as soon we registered, they sent-- after three days, they mobilized my brother-in-law. And they sent him away to-- to Stalinsk, Kuznetsk. And that-- I was left with my sister. So the-- the two of us, we started to look for work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we found an artel. That's, you know, like a-- a factory. But everything belonged to the government. And they've been making for the soldiers these quilted short coats. I didn't know sewing, my sister knew so. And what it was, it-- that wasn't any factories there, it was very hard to get work. Because she was a-- a soldier's wife she had the privileges. And we got into a factory where there was invalids, it was-- how you say? [RUSSIAN]. Dumb. And-- Like a sheltered workshop, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And so we-- we were the healthy. It was a few people only which could talk. And anyway, we took the-- a process where it went by conveyor. So my sister said, you know what? To earn more money, we will take the bigger.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the whole thing came big, to sew in the sleeves. And so because one was doing only the collar, one was doing the-- the, you know. And we took it the faster-- to earn a little bit more money. She put the child in a internot for a whole week, so she didn't see the child the whole week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we-- we were working. So it's was about eight months--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jewish people working with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was another couple, which-- Polish couple-- I mean, two women-- which came before. They were Communists in Poland and the Communist party sent them over the border. And they were-- they were two sister-in-laws, but their husbands, in 1937, was in Russia. They called it the [? chitska, ?] that they cleared up-- cleaned up. They didn't trust these Communists. And their husbands were sent to Siberia. So was-- they-- they'd been working there. Otherwise, later-- later, my brother-in-law sent my sister a-- a visa. And he-- as a wife, she could come over. So she went over, and they didn't want to give it to me. And I went to this commander. I went so many times. And I cried.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you couldn't understand that this one blanket what we have, we are sharing. She has a child. If she takes this blanket, I'm left with nothing. And he told me off. He said, what do you think you are? In-- in the-- in the [POLISH] Polish, and you come and cry. And-- and-- So I was left alone. I was living in this place. It was an old Russian woman. It wasn't-- she didn't have any coal. She was always sitting in her coat and these boots, warm boots.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to come-- I used to work in winter. I used to work-- go to work, it was dark because the night-- the day is short. And I used to come back. One day, I-- whatever I worn I put on-- on this bed. It wasn't a bed, it was only boards. Whatever I worn, I put it and on that I slept. And once she wanted to-- to make herself, you know, hot water or something, so she took it away half of the board. I came in the dark, I-- I looked. So I was sleeping only on half a board.

speaker: interviewee
text: So later, I-- I told these two women about my story. I didn't know anybody. I couldn't, because it was dark. I went, and I didn't know not a soul. And when I told these two women, they took me into-- to their place. And with one, she took me into her bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there. But what it was that their husbands were sent away. And one didn't know at all from. But the other one knew that her husband had 10 years. He was sent far to Siberia, where half-- half a year is day and half night.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day I received a-- a call to come to the undercover there. At that point, I thought I will commit suicide. And they ask me questions, and they ask me to spy on these women. And this, could-- could I do it? And they made me every-- every two months, every month, I don't remember, to meet them there. And I couldn't even tell a soul. They told me not to tell anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and she used to receive letters, but the letters, you know, he couldn't. So he was writing. Once she was reading a letter that it is-- they picked up from the field, and it was a big, a good year, and-- and the people are dancing and happy that they, you know, from the-- And-- and I was telling him these things. And then 10 years was over, and he wrote a letter that he wrote-- signed, that he will be working there till the war will be over. And every time I had to meet this man, I'm telling you, my-- I-- I thought I'm going to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name  of the lady-- the women, the two women?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Sonia, one was Gucia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their surnames?

speaker: interviewee
text: But their surnames, I don't remember. You know, they did so much for me, and I-- I couldn't. So I used to order a telephone call. You have to go to the post office to speak to my sister. And I couldn't tell her. So every time till the call came through, I started to cry on this side. She started, and the call was over the few minutes. And-- and one day it happened, a miracle. I came where this officer, the undercover there, asked me to meet him, and I asked for his name. And there they said, he's not here. And-- and he disappeared. And that was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Tomsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomsk, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed till the end of-- '40-- till the end of '42 or '43. I don't-- I don't know about the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there is a different story. My brother-in-law paid to-- me to bring me over.

speaker: interviewer
text: To?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to Stalinsk. Because I couldn't get-- you know, from work, couldn't get a-- how you say, relieved. And I-- I couldn't get a-- a ticket. I couldn't get a-- the-- the-- I had to have a visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop there.
